title: Monthly Decision Maker Panel data - March 2026
summary: The Bank of England's March 2026 Decision Maker Panel survey of 2,004 UK CFOs shows realized own-price growth at 3.7% (down 0.1pp), while year-ahead own-price inflation expectations ticked up to 3.5% in the three-month average. Year-ahead CPI expectations were unchanged in three-month averages but rose 0.5pp to 3.5% in single-month data, attributed to recent energy price increases. Overall business uncertainty jumped 10 percentage points to 57% reporting high or very high uncertainty.
key_points: Realized annual own-price growth was 3.7% in three months to March, down 0.1pp from February
Year-ahead own-price inflation expectations rose to 3.5% (three-month average), up 0.1pp from February; single-month data increased from 3.4% to 3.7%
Year-ahead CPI inflation expectations were unchanged in three-month averages but increased 0.5pp to 3.5% in single-month data, linked to energy price increases
Three-year-ahead CPI inflation expectations fell to 2.7% in three-month average (down 0.1pp) but rose 0.1pp in single-month data
Overall uncertainty rose sharply with 57% of firms reporting high/very high uncertainty, up 10pp from February
Realized employment growth was -0.3% in three months to March, down from -0.2% in February; year-ahead expectations unchanged at 0.1%
why_it_matters: This survey provides the Bank of England's primary channel for tracking real-time business inflation expectations and uncertainty in the UK economy. The sharp rise in uncertainty (+10pp) and upward drift in near-term inflation expectations (both own-price and CPI) could influence MPC members' views on the appropriate stance of monetary policy, particularly if energy price pressures continue to feed through to business pricing decisions.
macro_context: The survey indicates UK businesses are facing elevated uncertainty and expect near-term inflation to remain above the 2% target. The upward revision in single-month CPI expectations to 3.5% (from 3.0%) reflects passthrough from recent energy price increases. Employment growth expectations remain weak at 0.1% annually, suggesting labour market conditions may be softening. Three-year-ahead CPI expectations at 2.7% suggest businesses still expect inflation to moderate but remain above target over the medium term.

issues_found: Typo in important_numbers: '2004' should be '2004' (2,004 responses). This appears to be a truncated sample size - the source clearly states 2,004 respondents.

notes: Survey conducted 6-20 March 2026. Source is a legitimate Bank of England statistical release with good sample size (2,004 responses). The divergence between three-month averages and single-month data is notable—the single-month data shows a sharper uptick in inflation expectations. Published 02 April 2026 per body text, or 05 March 2026 per metadata; body text takes precedence as the authoritative date.
